back: The Pigeonhole Principle states that if n items are put into m containers, with n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item.

front: Define a pigeonhole in the context of the Pigeonhole Principle
back: A pigeonhole refers to a container or a bin where items are placed. In the context of the principle, it represents a partition or a subset of a set.

front: What is an example application of the Pigeonhole Principle?
back: One example is a classroom with 367 students and 30 possible birthdays (excluding February 29th for simplicity). The Pigeonhole Principle guarantees that at least two students share the same birthday, as there are more students (pigeons) than possible birthdays (holes).

front: What does the Pigeonhole Principle imply about functions?
back: The Pigeonhole Principle implies that if a function maps a larger set to a smaller set, it cannot be one-to-one (injective), meaning at least two elements in the domain must map to the same element in the codomain.

front: Is the Pigeonhole Principle limited to finite sets?
back: Yes, the Pigeonhole Principle is primarily applied to finite sets. It does not directly apply to infinite sets because the concept of 'more items than containers' does not translate in the same way when dealing with infinite quantities.
